name: Marie Van Brittan Brown Home Security System
short_description: The first patented residential television-surveillance system, combining four peepholes, a sliding camera, a monitor, two-way audio, and a remote door latch — filed in 1966, granted in 1969.
long_description: Designed by nurse Marie Van Brittan Brown and her husband Albert L. Brown, an electronics technician, this system was born out of safety concerns in their Jamaica, Queens neighborhood in the mid-1960s. A motorized camera slid vertically across four peepholes on the front door, transmitting images to a television monitor inside. A built-in microphone and speaker enabled two-way conversation without opening the door, while a remote solenoid latch let the occupant unlock the door from the safety of the interior. The system could also forward audio and video to a security center, and an alarm button alerted police. U.S. Patent 3,482,037 was filed August 1, 1966 and granted December 2, 1969; it was subsequently cited in 32 later patent applications. The invention established the conceptual blueprint — remote visual verification, two-way audio, and a remotely actuated door latch — that video doorbells and home-care cameras carry forward decades later.
ai_note: The system uses electromechanical components — motors, CCTV circuitry, a solenoid latch, radio transceiver — with no machine learning or AI inference of any kind.

images.caption: Patent diagram from U.S. Patent 3,482,037 (granted December 2, 1969) showing Marie Van Brittan Brown's home security system with camera, peepholes, monitor, and remote door control.
images.alt_text: A technical patent drawing showing a door fitted with four peepholes and a vertically sliding camera on the left, connected by dashed lines to a television monitor and control console on the right, representing Marie Van Brittan Brown's 1969 home security system.

sources.title: Marie Van Brittan Brown
sources.access_notes: Covers biography, patent filing date (August 1, 1966), grant date (December 2, 1969), patent number US 3,482,037, system components, and commercial obstacles.

sources.title: U.S. Patent 3,482,037 — Home Security System Utilizing Television Surveillance
sources.access_notes: Primary source. Lists inventors Marie Van Brittan Brown and Albert L. Brown; filing date August 1, 1966; grant date December 2, 1969. Describes camera, peepholes, monitor, two-way audio, solenoid door latch, and alarm capability.

sources.title: Marie van Brittan Brown
sources.access_notes: Notes four peepholes, sliding camera, TV monitors, two-way microphones, remote door unlock, police alarm, and that the patent was cited in 32 subsequent applications.

interactions.title: See who's at the door without opening it
interactions.description: When someone arrives, the occupant watches the visitor's picture appear on a television monitor inside the home, identifying them from a safe distance instead of unbolting the door.

interactions.title: Slide the camera to frame a visitor of any height
interactions.description: By pressing the up or down buttons on the control panel, the occupant drives the camera vertically across four peepholes, so a tall caller or a short one can be brought into view on the monitor.

interactions.title: Question the caller through the door
interactions.description: The occupant speaks into a microphone to ask the visitor their business and hears the reply over a loudspeaker, holding a full conversation without ever standing at the door.

interactions.title: Unlock the door from across the room
interactions.description: Satisfied the caller is welcome, the occupant presses a button that withdraws the bolt from its catch, releasing the solenoid lock and letting the visitor in from the safety of the interior.

interactions.title: Sound the alarm if a threat appears
interactions.description: If the occupant judges a real danger at the door, pressing the alarm button triggers an alarm at a guard station, a pushbutton route to summoning police or security.

interactions.title: A safer night shift for a nurse alone at home
interactions.description: Brown, a nurse keeping irregular hours in a high-crime part of Jamaica, Queens, built the system so she could assess and admit visitors herself when she was home alone, rather than open the door to an unknown caller.

interactions.title: Keep a record of who came to the door
interactions.description: Because the patent provides for forwarding the sound and picture to a security center or recording them, an occupant could later review a log of callers — an analog precursor to the saved clips of today's video doorbells.

interactions.title: Screen a delivery before granting entry
interactions.description: A resident expecting a package could watch the courier on the monitor, confirm through the two-way audio where to leave it, and release the door lock only once satisfied — all from inside, using the system's existing camera, intercom, and remote latch.
